summary: Gruber responds to criticism of his calm reaction to Apple's $200 iPhone price cut just two months after launch. He challenges the notion that the price cut proved Apple was gouging early adopters, arguing that fair pricing is whatever consumers willingly pay in an honest market, not a function of production costs. He distinguishes between something being unexpected and something being unfair. He acknowledges Apple didn't anticipate the backlash, noting the story dominated news coverage far beyond anything else Apple announced that day.
key_points: A fair price is determined by what consumers willingly pay in an honest, competitive market, not by production costs or profit margins
There is an important distinction between a price cut being unexpected and being unfair — the iPhone cut was the former, not the latter
Apple clearly misjudged both the customer backlash and the media frenzy the price cut would generate
The typical early-adopter bargain with Apple is trading sketchy quality control for maximum time before price cuts or obsolescence — this time that bargain broke down unusually fast
money_quotes: A fair price is whatever people are willing to pay in an honest, competitive market, and doesn't necessarily bear any relation to the cost to produce the good or service.
A company is under no moral obligation to limit themselves to some sort of maximum "reasonable" profit in the face of consumer demand that would bear a higher price.
There's a difference, though, between unexpected and unfair.
The downside of early adoption of Apple products is sometimes-sketchy quality control; the upside is that buying early maximizes the time before it's obsoleted or the price is cut.
I don't know, I can imagine quite a bit.
News regarding the price cut — and the reaction from angered iPhone customers — far overshadowed coverage of anything else Apple announced on Wednesday.
key_insight: A dramatic price cut may be unexpected and even strategically unwise, but charging what the market will bear is not unfair — the distinction between surprise and injustice matters.
